In this paper, we introduce Cinemo, a novel image animation approach that aims at achieving better appearance consistency and motion smoothness. Extensive experiments compared with several state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our pro- posed approach.

limitation_expression: However, preserving appearance consistency to the static input image, and avoiding abrupt motion change in the generated animation, remains challenging.

Previous I2V approaches are usually limited in their gen- eralization and effective primarily in specific domains, such as human hair [ 66], talking heads [ 13, 57, 58], and hu- man bodies [ 2, 3, 47, 58, 60, 61], etc.
2, existing methods still encounter two major challenges: appearance consis- tency and motion smoothness.
However, this balance remains a persistent dilemma.
method_expression: Firstly, to better preserve the appearance consis- tency across frames, we propose to learn the distribution of the differences (motion residual) between frames, instead of direct learning on the multiple frames themselves.

Secondly, we propose to utilize the low-frequency Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) components of the input image, as the layout guidance to suppress the abrupt or undesired motion during the inference phase.
To avoid over-suppression of motion dynamics, we propose a new design of dynamics degree con- trol inspired by [10] that enables users to explicitly control the degree of motion.

We summarize our contributions as follows: • We propose Cinemo, a diffusion-based image animation model that learns the distribution of motion residuals across frames rather than directly predicting the multiple frames themselves, in turn better preserves the appearance consistency. • To mitigate the abrupt or undesired motion changes, we utilize the low-frequency DCT component of the input image, to initialize the inference noise during the inference phase.
During the inference, we propose DCTInit, which utilizes the low-frequency components of DCT coef- ficients of the static input image to refine the initial noise and stabilize the generation process.
experiment_expression: Extensive experiments compared with several state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our pro- posed approach.
In the end, we also demonstrate how our model can be applied for motion transfer or video editing of any given video.

We also demonstrate our model can be easily extended to other applications such as video editing and motion transfer.

We conduct comprehensive quantita- tive and qualitative experiments and demonstrate that our model achieves the best appearance consistency and motion smoothness, without over-suppressing the motion dynamics.
Our experimental re- sults demonstrate the effectiveness and the superiority of our approach compared to existing methods.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Diffusion models have achieved significant progress in the
task of image animation due to their powerful generative
capabilities. However, preserving appearance consistency to
the static input image, and avoiding abrupt motion change in
the generated animation, remains challenging. In this paper,
we introduce Cinemo, a novel image animation approach
that aims at achieving better appearance consistency and
motion smoothness. The core of Cinemo is to focus on learn-
ing the distribution of motion residuals, rather than directly
predicting frames as in existing diffusion models. During the
inference, we further mitigate the sudden motion changes
in the generated video by introducing a novel DCT-based
noise refinement strategy. To counteract the over-smoothing
‡ Corresponding authors. † Intern at Shanghai AI Lab. § Project lead.
of motion, we introduce a dynamics degree control design
for better control of the magnitude of motion. Altogether,
these strategies enable Cinemo to produce highly consis-
tent, smooth, and motion-controllable results. Extensive
experiments compared with several state-of-the-art methods
demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our pro-
posed approach. In the end, we also demonstrate how our
model can be applied for motion transfer or video editing of
any given video. The project page is available at https:
//maxin-cn.github.io/cinemo_project/.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Image animation, also known as Image-to-Video generation
(I2V), “animates” a given static image to transform it into
a video sequence, with the goal of preserving the original
content but injecting realistic and temporally coherent dy-
namics. Fig. 1 demonstrates some examples (please visit
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
7288

(a) Appearance Inconsistency (Prompt: windmill turning)
(b) Motion Roughness (Prompt: car moving)
Input Image
Input Image
Figure 2. Appearance consistency and motion smoothness. (a) Upper row: appearance changes over time (result from PIA [74]). (b)
Lower row: the car takes a sudden turnaround (result from SEINE [8]).
the project page to visualize the motion). It is an engaging
and convenient way of creating video content, which can be
directly used in real-world video production.
Previous I2V approaches are usually limited in their gen-
eralization and effective primarily in specific domains, such
as human hair [ 66], talking heads [ 13, 57, 58], and hu-
man bodies [ 2, 3, 47, 58, 60, 61], etc. Recent advance-
ments in large-scale diffusion models have enabled gener-
alization to open domains, demonstrating success in image
[7, 36, 43], 3D geometry [ 27, 28, 37], and video genera-
tion [ 5, 15, 31, 32, 48, 59]. In particular, extending the
video diffusion models to image animation has received
much attention [8, 10, 42, 67, 73, 74], aiming to utilize the
powerful spatio-temporal generation priors. Despite these
advancements, as demonstrated in Fig. 2, existing methods
still encounter two major challenges: appearance consis-
tency and motion smoothness. Firstly, the generated videos
may fail to maintain the visual appearance over time (the
overall color tone and the shape of the windmill in Fig. 2(a)).
Secondly, abrupt motion or deformation of objects may hap-
pen as shown in Fig. 2(b). One common way to maintain the
above consistencies is to reduce the motion dynamics.
Clearly, it is more desirable to maintain the appearance
consistency and motion smoothness, without sacrificing
large dynamic motion. However, this balance remains a
persistent dilemma. In this paper, we aim to strike such a
balance, by proposing two novel designs in our model, Cin-
emo, which is based on a foundational text-to-video (T2V)
model [59]. Firstly, to better preserve the appearance consis-
tency across frames, we propose to learn the distribution of
the differences (motion residual) between frames, instead of
direct learning on the multiple frames themselves. Secondly,
we propose to utilize the low-frequency Discrete Cosine
Transform (DCT) components of the input image, as the
layout guidance to suppress the abrupt or undesired motion
during the inference phase. Such process (DCTInit) is able
to address the noise discrepancy issue between training and
inference phases as mentioned in other works [ 34, 46, 64]
and effective in suppressing the abrupt motion as demon-
strated in Fig. 6(c&d). To avoid over-suppression of motion
dynamics, we propose a new design of dynamics degree con-
trol inspired by [10] that enables users to explicitly control
the degree of motion.
With the above novel strategies, our model can produce
highly appearance- and motion-consistent videos that align
with the prompts. We also demonstrate our model can be
easily extended to other applications such as video editing
and motion transfer. We conduct comprehensive quantita-
tive and qualitative experiments and demonstrate that our
model achieves the best appearance consistency and motion
smoothness, without over-suppressing the motion dynamics.
We summarize our contributions as follows:
• We propose Cinemo, a diffusion-based image animation
model that learns the distribution of motion residuals
across frames rather than directly predicting the multiple
frames themselves, in turn better preserves the appearance
consistency.
• To mitigate the abrupt or undesired motion changes, we
utilize the low-frequency DCT component of the input
image, to initialize the inference noise during the inference
phase.
raw_sections.method:
4.2. Comparisons with State-of-the-art Methods
Quantitative comparison. We conduct a comparative analy-
sis on the evaluation metrics FVD, IS, FID, and CLIPSIM be-
tween our model and the state-of-the-art methods including,
Gen-2, I2VGen-XL, DynamiCrafter, SEINE, ConsistI2V ,
and Follow-Your-Click on the UCF-101 and MSR-VTT
datasets. All quantitative experiments are conducted in a
zero-shot manner. From Tab. 1, our model achieves the high-
est scores across all five metrics, demonstrating its superior
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
We first outline the experimental setup, covering datasets,
baselines, evaluation metrics, and implementation details.
Following that, we compare the experimental results with
state-of-the-art methods. Finally, we present the analysis of
our model.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce Cinemo, a novel image animation
model that achieves better image consistency and motion
smoothness. Our core idea is to focus on learning the dis-
tribution of motion residuals rather than directly predicting
the next frames. During the inference, we propose DCTInit,
which utilizes the low-frequency components of DCT coef-
ficients of the static input image to refine the initial noise
and stabilize the generation process. Our experimental re-
sults demonstrate the effectiveness and the superiority of our
approach compared to existing methods.
